title: Productivity Growth and the Challenge of Real-Time Policymaking
summary: A conceptual, research-oriented speech on how shifts in trend productivity growth affect the economy and complicate real-time policymaking, motivated by the attention on AI. Williams stresses a 'recognition problem'—trend shifts are very hard to identify in real time, so expectations and behavior adjust only gradually—and shows that a productivity acceleration generates a sustained, if modest, disinflationary impulse and eventually raises the natural rate of interest. He draws no conclusions about the current stance or near-term policy path; instead he argues for robust policy approaches given the large uncertainty around structural change. The monetary-policy read is therefore indirect: it leans toward humility and caution rather than a hawkish or dovish bias.

key_quotes.quote: In addition to the effects on growth, an increase in productivity growth also reduces costs that businesses face, contributing to downward pressure on inflation until prices and wages fully adjust.
key_quotes.context: Explaining that a productivity acceleration is disinflationary, a structural reason inflation could ease on its own

key_quotes.quote: Second, an increase in trend productivity growth raises real interest rates in the long run.
key_quotes.context: On the implications of a productivity boom for the natural rate of interest / r-star

key_quotes.quote: Therefore, as in the case of any type of structural change, robust policy approaches have an especially favorable cost-benefit calculus.
key_quotes.context: Closing argument for cautious, robust policy under deep uncertainty about productivity trends
